fuzzy_prd:
We need to complete the OpenAPI contract code-generation behavior covered by the tests. The scope includes YAML parsing edge cases, quoted enum preservation, anchor/alias detection, alias enum resolution, OpenAPI encoding validation, primitive model serialization/deserialization, enum JSON mapping, unique array handling, map serialization, HTTP client request mapping, HTTP client result mapping, and server routing. The adapter should route by feature and produce deterministic summaries of generated/model behavior.

One extra detail on the YAML enum piece because that was the main point of confusion: YAML will happily treat certain unquoted uppercase values like 'NO', 'YES', 'TRUE', 'FALSE' as booleans or other special tokens. For this contract, if we want those to stay JSON strings, the enum entries in the YAML source need to be quoted, like '- "NO"', and the parser behavior needs to keep them as strings instead of coercing them. That needs to be true both on a straight read and on a tree merge, and the expected result stays is_array=true and first_value_json="NO", including the quotes in the JSON rendering.

Also on the reusable alias detection, we should only say contains_anchor_and_alias=true when the raw YAML text contains both an anchor token with '&' and an alias token with '*'. If there is only an anchor, only an alias, or neither, it should be contains_anchor_and_alias=false. This check needs to happen from the raw text rather than the parsed structure, since by the time parsing is done the alias may already be resolved and that signal is gone.

For the case where a property's enum is provided through a YAML alias like 'enum: *status_values', we are keeping the current behavior as-is: the property-level resolved enum list comes back empty, so the expected output is exactly 'enum_values=\n'. That is intentional for now even though the YAML itself is valid and the anchor resolution works.

One other validation note: an OpenAPI 3.x requestBody can have an 'encoding' block under a 'content' entry, and if that encoding sets a contentType for a binary property, we should still accept the document. The expected result there is is_valid=true, so the validator should not reject it just because request-body encoding metadata appears next to a binary-format property schema.

And just to tie this back to the existing scope, this same YAML quote-preservation behavior is the feature1_1_yaml_quoted_enum path and it applies to both 'read' and 'merge' on YAML trees, specifically the behavior where the x-extensible-enum values need to keep quoted strings like "NO" after both operations.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with feature) -> stdout OpenAPI parsing/model/client/server result lines

trigger_keywords: no content response not surfaced
204 result silently fails
unit result for empty body response
HTTP client result with no body
no_content operation result mapping
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an HTTP client operation returns a 204 No Content response (or any declared no-body success response), the result mapping layer must surface a successful result explicitly. The expected output for such a case is: result=success\ndata=unit\n — meaning the result is marked as success and the data field is reported as the literal string 'unit', indicating an intentional empty/void payload rather than a missing or failed response.

trigger_keywords: datetime serialization format
date-time deserialized with offset format
stringDateTime output format after parsing
timestamp loses seconds on deserialization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a date-time string is deserialized from JSON, the output format may differ slightly from the input. Specifically, '2024-11-04T12:00:00Z' (with explicit zero seconds) should deserialize and print as '2024-11-04T12:00Z' (without the :00 seconds component). This is the expected normalized output format for the stringDateTime field in the deserialization contract.

trigger_keywords: high precision decimal number serialization
BigDecimal number field wire value
number field retains full precision in JSON
109288282772724 long decimal preserved
number type vs float double precision
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A model field typed as 'number' (not float or double) must serialize and deserialize with full decimal precision. The value 109288282772724.4225837838838383888 must appear exactly as-is in both the serialized JSON output and the deserialized printed output. This field is distinct from 'numberFloat' (1.23) and 'numberDouble' (4.56) which use lower-precision floating point types. The 'number' type must use a high-precision representation such as BigDecimal.

trigger_keywords: unique array deduplication order
set semantics on array field
duplicate removal preserves first occurrence
unique_objects array behavior
uniqueItems deserialization contract
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Arrays declared as unique collections (uniqueItems: true in the schema) must remove duplicate entries during deserialization while preserving the order of first occurrence for each unique value. Arrays not declared unique must retain all supplied entries including duplicates. In the test cases provided, the unique_objects and unique_ints arrays have no duplicates in the input, so the output simply reflects all elements in original order.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: whatever pattern we established for the HTTP client result contracts
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The HTTP client result mapping contract defined in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature3_2_http_client_result_mapping.json, specifically the 'no_content' operation case that expects result=success and data=unit for a 204 response, and the 'create_item' case that expects deserialized body fields to be printed individually.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more specific details on that aspect — you'd need to check the existing test contracts in the rcb_tests/public_test_cases directory and cross-reference with the component that handles that part of the pipeline. The PRD in start.md should have the authoritative behavior description if the test cases don't fully clarify it.

feature1_1_yaml_quoted_enum.json.description: YAML parsing and YAML tree merging preserve quoted string enum values as JSON strings instead of converting them to bare tokens.

feature1_2_yaml_anchor_alias_detection.j.description: YAML text is classified as using reusable anchors only when at least one anchor definition and one alias reference are both present.

openapi: "3.0.0"

  version: "1.0"
paths: {}

    AnchorOwner:
      type: object
      properties:
        status:
          type: string
          enum: &status_values
            - Active
            - Inactive
    AliasConsumer:
      type: object
      properties:
        status:
      type: string
      enum: *status_values

            - Active

feature1_4_openapi_encoding_validation.j.description: An OpenAPI document with request-body encoding metadata for a binary property is accepted as a valid API description.

input.yaml:
openapi: 3.0.1
info:
  title: Sample API
  version: "1.0"
paths:
  /endpoint:
    post:
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              type: object
              properties:
                image:
                  type: string
                  format: binary
            encoding:
              image:
                contentType: image/png, image/jpeg, image/webp
      responses:
        200:
          description: Everything OK

feature2_2_primitive_json_deserializatio.description: JSON containing primitive, string-format, binary, numeric, and array values deserializes into values without losing precision, identity, or decoded binary length.

feature2_3_enum_json_mapping.json.description: Enum-like model fields serialize to and deserialize from their defined JSON wire values, including extensible string values and arrays of enum values.

feature2_4_unique_array_deserialization..description: Arrays declared as unique collections deserialize with duplicate entries removed while preserving first-seen order; non-unique arrays preserve all supplied entries.

feature2_5_map_json_serialization.json.description: String maps, typed object maps, nested object maps, and unknown-value maps serialize as JSON objects while retaining dynamic keys and nested value structure.

feature3_1_http_client_request_mapping.j.description: Generated HTTP clients translate operation inputs into real HTTP requests with the expected method, path, query parameters, headers, and JSON body.

feature3_2_http_client_result_mapping.js.description: Generated HTTP clients convert HTTP responses into success or failure results using status code, status text, optional body content, and deserialization outcome.

feature3_3_http_server_routing.json.description: Generated server routing parses request bodies, headers, and query parameters, returns declared response status and body content, and surfaces bad request or missing response statuses through the HTTP layer.
